questions.question: What is the key reason why avoidance strengthens anxiety in the long run?

options.text: Avoidance makes you physically weaker
Your brain learns 'avoidance = safety,' making the next encounter feel even more threatening
Avoidance causes chemical imbalances
People judge you for avoiding things

questions.explanation: When you avoid something and anxiety decreases, your brain learns that avoidance equals safety. This reinforces the pattern and makes the next encounter feel even scarier, creating a cycle that shrinks your life over time.

questions.question: What does 'intolerance of uncertainty' mean in the context of anxiety?

options.text: Being allergic to unpredictable situations
Struggling with uncertainty itself, needing more and more certainty to feel safe
Not being able to make any decisions
Preferring spontaneous activities

questions.explanation: Intolerance of uncertainty means struggling with the experience of not knowing what will happen. People use worry to try to reduce uncertainty, but this actually increases sensitivity to uncertainty over time, requiring more and more certainty to feel safe.

questions.question: What is the difference between productive and unproductive worry?

options.text: Productive worry is longer; unproductive worry is shorter
Productive worry leads to action and a clear endpoint; unproductive worry just loops and increases anxiety
Productive worry happens during the day; unproductive worry happens at night
There is no difference—all worry is harmful

questions.explanation: Productive worry identifies a real, solvable problem, leads to a specific action plan, has a clear endpoint, and results in reduced anxiety. Unproductive worry focuses on things outside your control, generates more questions than answers, has no endpoint, and increases anxiety.

questions.question: What is 'catastrophic chaining' in the worry cycle?

options.text: A helpful planning technique
When each 'what if' thought triggers another, leading to increasingly extreme scenarios
A type of breathing exercise
Connecting positive thoughts together

questions.explanation: Catastrophic chaining occurs when each worry spawns another, increasingly extreme scenario: 'What if I fail?' → 'What if I get fired?' → 'What if I can't pay rent?' → 'What if I lose everything?' This chain of 'what ifs' fuels anxiety without leading to solutions.

questions.question: According to the Worry Decision Tree, what should you do if something is NOT within your control?

options.text: Worry harder until you find a solution
Acknowledge the uncertainty, practice acceptance, and redirect attention to the present
Avoid thinking about it completely
Ask others for reassurance repeatedly

questions.explanation: When something is outside your control, the healthy response is to acknowledge the uncertainty, practice acceptance that you cannot control everything, and redirect your attention to the present moment rather than continuing to worry.

title: Worry Patterns and the Anxiety Cycle - Quiz
